sn35.117:1.1: “Monks, before my awakening, when I was still just an unawakened bodhisatta, the thought occurred to me:
sn35.117:1.2: ‘Those five strings of sensuality that previously made contact with my awareness—that are past, ceased, changed: My mind, having often gone there, might go to those that are present, or occasionally to those that are future.’
sn35.117:1.3: Then the thought occurred to me:
sn35.117:1.4: ‘Those five strings of sensuality that previously made contact with my awareness—that are past, ceased, changed: There, for my own sake, heedfulness, mindfulness, and a protection of my awareness should be practiced.
sn35.117:1.5: “Therefore, monks, those five strings of sensuality that previously made contact with your awareness, too—that are past, ceased, changed: Your mind, having often gone there, might go to those that are present, or occasionally to those that are future.
sn35.117:1.6: Therefore, those five strings of sensuality that previously made contact with your awareness, too—that are past, ceased, changed: There, for your own sake, heedfulness, mindfulness, and a protection of your awareness should be practiced.
sn35.117:1.7: “Therefore, monks, that dimension should be experienced where the eye [vision] ceases and the perception [mental label] of form fades.
sn35.117:1.8: That dimension should be experienced where the tongue ceases and the perception of flavor fades.//
sn35.117:1.9: That dimension should be experienced where the intellect ceases and the perception of idea fades. That dimension should be experienced.”
sn35.117:1.10: Having said this, the Blessed One got up from his seat and went into his dwelling.
sn35.117:2.1: Then, not long after the Blessed One had left, this thought occurred to the monks:
sn35.117:2.2: “This brief statement the Blessed One made, after which he went into his dwelling without analyzing the detailed meaning—i.e.,
sn35.117:2.3: ‘Therefore, monks, that dimension should be experienced where the eye ceases and the perception of form fades...
sn35.117:2.4: where the tongue ceases and the perception of flavor fades…
sn35.117:2.5: where the intellect ceases and the perception of idea fades: That dimension should be experienced’:
sn35.117:2.6: now who might analyze the unanalyzed detailed meaning of this brief statement?”
sn35.117:3.1: Then the thought occurred to them,
sn35.117:3.2: “Ven. Ānanda is praised by the Teacher and esteemed by his observant companions in the holy life.
sn35.117:3.3: He is capable of analyzing the unanalyzed detailed meaning of this brief statement. Suppose we were to go to him and, on arrival, cross-question him about this matter.”
sn35.117:3.4: So the monks went to Ven. Ānanda and, on arrival, exchanged courteous greetings with him.
sn35.117:4.1: After an exchange of friendly greetings & courtesies, they sat to one side.
sn35.117:4.2: As they were sitting there, they [told him what had happened, and added,]
sn35.117:6.1: [He replied:] “Friends, it’s as if a man needing heartwood, looking for heartwood, wandering in search of heartwood—passing over the root & trunk of a standing tree possessing heartwood—were to imagine that heartwood should be sought among its branches & leaves. So it is with you, who—having bypassed the Blessed One when you were face to face with him, the Teacher—imagine that I should be asked about this matter. For knowing, the Blessed One knows; seeing, he sees. He is the Eye, he is Knowledge, he is Dhamma, he is Brahmā. He is the speaker, the proclaimer, the elucidator of meaning, the giver of the deathless, the lord of the Dhamma, the Tathāgata. That was the time when you should have cross-questioned him about this matter. However he answered, that was how you should have remembered it.” “Yes, friend Ānanda: Knowing, the Blessed One knows; seeing, he sees. He is the Eye, he is Knowledge, he is Dhamma, he is Brahmā. He is the speaker, the proclaimer, the elucidator of meaning, the giver of the deathless, the lord of the Dhamma, the Tathāgata. That was the time when we should have cross-questioned him about this matter. However he answered, that was how we should have remembered it. But you are praised by the Teacher and esteemed by your observant companions in the holy life. You are capable of analyzing the unanalyzed detailed meaning of this brief statement.
sn35.117:6.2: Analyze the meaning, Ven. Ānanda without making it difficult!”
sn35.117:7.1: “In that case, my friends, listen & pay close attention. I will speak.”
sn35.117:7.2: “As you say, friend,” the monks responded to him.
sn35.117:8.1: “Friends, concerning the brief statement the Blessed One made, after which he went into his dwelling without analyzing the detailed meaning—i.e.,
sn35.117:8.2: ‘Therefore, monks, that dimension should be experienced where the eye ceases and the perception of form fades.
sn35.117:8.3: where the intellect ceases and the perception of idea fades. That dimension should be experienced’—
sn35.117:8.4: I understand the detailed meaning to be this:
sn35.117:8.5: This was stated by the Blessed One with regard to the cessation of the six sense media.”
sn35.117:8.6: “So, concerning the brief statement the Blessed One made, after which he entered his dwelling without analyzing the detailed meaning—i.e.,
sn35.117:8.7: where the intellect ceases and the perception of idea fades. That dimension should be experienced’—
sn35.117:8.8: this is how I understand the detailed meaning.
sn35.117:8.12: Now, friends, if you wish, having gone to the Blessed One,
sn35.117:8.14: However he answers is how you should remember it.”
sn35.117:9.1: Then the monks, delighting in & approving of Ven. Ānanda’s words, got up from their seats and went to the Blessed One. On arrival, having bowed down to him, they sat to one side. As they were sitting there, they (told him what had happened after he had gone into his dwelling, and ended by saying,)
sn35.117:10.14: “Then Ven. Ānanda analyzed the meaning using these words, these statements, these phrases.”
sn35.117:11.1: “Ānanda is wise, monks. Ānanda is a person of great discernment.
sn35.117:11.2: If you had asked me about this matter, I too would have answered in the same way he did.
sn35.117:11.3: That is its meaning, and that is how you should remember it.”
